title: Today's AI Brief: So you’ve heard these AI terms and nodded along; let’s fix that
summary: A look at the latest AI terms and their practical applications
imageAlt: Image from source article for So you’ve heard these AI terms and nodded along; let’s fix that
imageCaption: So you’ve heard these AI terms and nodded along; let’s fix that
imageSourceTitle: So you’ve heard these AI terms and nodded along; let’s fix that

body.text: Artificial intelligence is changing the world and bringing a whole new language with it. I was checking out a recent article from TechCrunch that tries to decode some of the most common AI terms. You might have heard of LLMs, RAG, and RLHF, but do you know what they actually mean? I've been trying to wrap my head around these terms, and it's amazing how quickly they evolve.

The article from TechCrunch provides a helpful guide to understanding AI terminology. It seems like every five minutes, there's a new term popping up. My read is that it's easy to feel left behind, but there's no need to be an expert to grasp the basics. For instance, I came across an example of how Braintrust uses Codex with GPT-5.5 to turn customer requests into code. Essentially, they're leveraging AI to speed up their experimentation and coding process.

What stands out to me is how this could change the way teams work and collaborate. Imagine being able to generate code faster and more efficiently - it's a game-changer. And then there's the fascinating topic of 'hallucinations' in AI. It's when an AI model generates information that isn't actually there. It's a bit like a creative writing exercise gone wrong. I guess you could say AI has its own version of writer's block... or maybe just 'hallucination' block.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
